coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical immunosensor with graphene quantum dots and apoferritin-encapsulated Cu nanoparticles double-assisted signal amplification for detection of avian leukosis virus subgroup J
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown copyright © 2013 Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xindong
Chen, Lijian
Su, Xiurong
Ai, Shiyun
coredata.dc:description: A novel sandwich electrochemical immunoassay was developed for ultrasensitive detection of avian leukosis virus subgroup J (ALVs-J) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and apoferritin-encapsulated Cu (Cu-apoferritin) nanoparticles for signal amplification. GQDs were used both for the conjugation of primary ALVs-J antibodies (Ab1), and immobilization of secondary ALVs-J antibodies (Ab2) after compounded with Fe3O4. Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles were first selected to immobilize onto Fe3O4@GQDs hybrid as electroactive probes. After the well-known sandwich-type assembly, Cu was released from the apoferritin cavity, and then detected by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). Owing to the huge surface area GQDs provided, a considerable number of antibodies were loaded onto the immunosensor, which effectively increased the electrical signal. And the introduction of Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles increased the loading amount of electroactive probes significantly; hence the signal was once again amplified. To embody the signal amplification property of the protocol, the performance of various labels was compared in detail. The immunosensor displayed excellent analytical performance for the detection of ALVs-J range from 102.08 to 104.50 TCID50/mL with a detection limit of 115TCID50/mL (S/N=3), and the resulting immunosensor also showed high sensitivity, good reproducibility and stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Apoferritin-encapsulated Cu nanoparticles
Fe3O4@graphene quantum dots hybrid
Immunosensor
Signal amplification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of Fe3O4@GQDs hybrid and Cu-apoferritin
Comparison of different signal amplification strategies
Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the immunosensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of GQDs (A), Fe3O4 (B), Fe3O4@GQDs (C) and Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles (D). FT-IR spectroscopy of GQDs (E) and amino-functional Fe3O4 (F).

CVs for bare GCE (a), GQDs (b), Ab1/GQDs (c), BSA/Ab1/GQDs (d), and ALVs-J/BSA/Ab1/GQDs (e). Modified GCE in 5mM Fe
(
CN
)
6
3 − / 4 −
(1:1) solution containing 0.1M KCl in potential range of −0.2 to 0.7V with the scan rate of 100mVs−1. Inset: CVs for bare GCE (a) and GQDs modified GCE (b) in 5mM hexaammineruthenium(III) chloride solution in potential range of −0.35 to 0.2V with the scan rate of 100mVs−1.
The DPV comparisons for the proposed immunosensors with (upper curve) and without (lower curve) GQDs (A) and apoferritin (B).

(A) DPV responses of the immunosensor with increasing ALVs-J concentrations under the optimized conditions (102.08, 102.25, 102.55, 102.75, 103.00, 103.35, 103.65, 104.15, 104.50 TCID50/mL). (B) Calibration curve. (C) Effect of accumulation time. (D) Effect of accumulation potential.
(A) Selectivity of the immunosensor for ALVs-J, ALVs-A and AREV. (B) Reproducibility of six immunosensors towards 104.15 TCID50/mL ALVs-J.
Schematic representation of the preparation of Fe3O4@GQDs/Ab2–Cu-apoferritin/BSA (A) and immunosensor (B).

head.ce:title: Electrochemical immunosensor with graphene quantum dots and apoferritin-encapsulated Cu nanoparticles double-assisted signal amplification for detection of avian leukosis virus subgroup J

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Shandong Agricultural University, Taian, Shandong 271018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel sandwich electrochemical immunoassay was developed for ultrasensitive detection of avian leukosis virus subgroup J (ALVs-J) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and apoferritin-encapsulated Cu (Cu-apoferritin) nanoparticles for signal amplification. GQDs were used both for the conjugation of primary ALVs-J antibodies (Ab1), and immobilization of secondary ALVs-J antibodies (Ab2) after compounded with Fe3O4. Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles were first selected to immobilize onto Fe3O4@GQDs hybrid as electroactive probes. After the well-known sandwich-type assembly, Cu was released from the apoferritin cavity, and then detected by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). Owing to the huge surface area GQDs provided, a considerable number of antibodies were loaded onto the immunosensor, which effectively increased the electrical signal. And the introduction of Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles increased the loading amount of electroactive probes significantly; hence the signal was once again amplified. To embody the signal amplification property of the protocol, the performance of various labels was compared in detail. The immunosensor displayed excellent analytical performance for the detection of ALVs-J range from 102.08 to 104.50 TCID50/mL with a detection limit of 115TCID50/mL (S/N=3), and the resulting immunosensor also showed high sensitivity, good reproducibility and stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Avian leukosis viruses (ALVs) are the most common naturally occurring avian retroviruses associated with neoplastic diseases (Zhou et al., 2011). They are divided into 10 different viral subgroups (designated as A–J) based on their host range, viral envelope interference, and cross-neutralisation patterns (Yun et al., 2013). Avian leukosis virus subgroup J (ALVs-J), the most recently discovered avian exogenous retrovirus, is thought to have emerged via a recombination event between an unknown exogenous avian leukosis virus and an endogenous retrovirus (Pan et al., 2011). It was first isolated in 1989 from meat-type chickens, and diseases associated with ALVs-J have caused enormous economic losses in chickens worldwide since 1990s (Shang et al., 2012). Therefore, devices or methods for the detection of ALVs-J are particularly important. In recent years, several analytical methods have been reported for the detection of ALVs-J, including immunohistochemistry (Gharaibeh et al., 2001), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA; Yun et al., 2013), and surface plasmon resonance (SPR; Huang et al., 2006). However, these methods often require a few days to complete the detection. In contrast, electrochemical immunosensors (Munge et al., 2011; Ricci et al., 2012) with a sandwich-type structure are widely used due to their advantages in terms of simple pre-treatment, rapid detection, low cost, and high sensitivity.

Various types of elements have been used for signal amplification in electrochemical immunosensors, including nanoparticles (Li et al., 2011a), quantum dots (Zhang et al., 2012), and electroactive component-encapsulated nanoparticles (Du et al., 2011; Viswanathan et al., 2009). The use of Fe3O4 nanospheres has motivated great interest due to their advantages such as narrow size distributions, large surface areas (for maximal protein or enzyme binding), controllable size, and the possibility to be functionalized easily (Deng et al., 2005; Zhang and Zheng, 2012). Previously, our group designed an electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of ALVs-J. The design was based on multifunctional Fe3O4 core/Ni-Al layered double hydroxides shell nanospheres. (Shang et al., 2012), and the Fe3O4 core exhibited good performance and a great convenience for separating. In addition, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are mainly distributed from 3 to 20nm in size, have also been extensively studied because of low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility and high specific surface area (Pan et al., 2010; Shen et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2012). As compared to other carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and carbon nanotube, GQDs possess a larger number of carboxyl groups, much smaller volumes, and have a good electrical conductivity. All these characters make GQDs ideal materials for the application in electrochemical immunosensor. Recently, numerous applications of GQDs have been developed, but most of them are focused on the optical properties, such as bioimaging and fluorescence (Jing et al., 2010; Li et al., 2011b; Yan et al., 2010; Zhu et al., 2011), and few examples in electrochemical immunosensor have been reported.

Herein, we designed a novel sandwich-type electrochemical immunosensor with Fe3O4@GQDs hybrid and apoferritin-encapsulated Cu (Cu-apoferritin) nanoparticles as labels for highly selective and sensitive detection of ALVs-J. Apoferritin consists of a spherical protein shell (around 12.5nm in diameter) composed of 24 subunits surrounding an aqueous cavity with a diameter of about 8nm that is capable of accommodating around 4500 iron atoms. The protein cage of apoferritin can be disassociated into 24 subunits at low pH (2.0) and reconstituted in a high pH (8.5) environment (Liu and Lin, 2007). Recently, apoferritin-encapsulated metallic nanoparticles were used for a highly sensitive electrochemical immunoassay of protein (Liu et al., 2006a, 2006b). Among these immunosensors, apoferritin-encapsulated cadmium and lead phosphates were widely used (Viswanathan et al., 2009; Liu et al., 2007). However, the employment of cadmium and lead could cause serious damage to people's health; as compared to cadmium and lead, copper was much less toxic and had a good stripping voltammetric signal. Therefore, Cu was selected as the electroactive probes in this paper. The more electroactive probes the immunosensor carries, the more sensitive the immunoassay. So we took advantage of the high loading capacity of apoferritin to accommodate a large amount of electroactive probes (Cu).

In view of the advantages of Fe3O4 nanospheres and GQDs, the Fe3O4@GQDs hybrid was synthesized as a substrate to link secondary antibodies (Ab2) and apoferritin. Furthermore, GQDs were used in the immunosensor platform to increase the surface area for capturing numerous primary antibodies (Ab1). The sensitivity of the immunosensor was greatly amplified by the following strategies: (i) quite a lot of electroactive probes was introduced into the immunosensor via the protein cage of apoferritin and (ii) high content of antibodies and Cu-apoferritin was combined through the large free room provided by GQDs. This assay approach showed a promising potential in clinical analysis.

Graphite powder was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Coomassie brilliant blue G250 was purchased from Basf Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Apoferritin and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were obtained from Sigma (USA). A serum specimen with ALVs-J and ALVs-J antibodies (Ab) were provided and purified by the College of Animal Science and Technology, Shandong Agricultural University (Taian, China). A 0.1M acetate buffer (pH 4.5) was prepared by mixing the stock solutions of 0.1M CH3COOH and 0.1M CH3COONa and adjusting the pH with 0.1M CH3COOH or 0.1M NaOH. A 0.1M phosphate buffered saline (PBS; pH 7.4) was prepared by mixing the stock solutions of 0.1M Na2HPO4 and 0.1M NaH2PO4 and adjusting the pH with 0.1M H3PO4 or 0.1M NaOH. Other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and all solutions were prepared using double distilled deionized water from quartz. All the reagents were used without further purification.

Electrochemical experiments were performed with a CHI660C electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Co., China) with a conventional three-electrode cell. A bare or modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE; CHI104, d=3mm) was used as working electrode. A saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a platinum wire were employed as reference electrode and auxiliary electrode, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 380 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo electron Co., USA). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained at a 100 CX II transmission electron microscope (JEM, Japan). Inductively coupled plasma (ICP) analysis was performed with a 700 series ICP-OES (Varian). All the measurements were carried out at room temperature (25.0±0.5°C).

GQDs were synthesized according to the following steps (Li et al., 2012). Firstly, graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummer's method (Narayanan et al., 2012). Subsequently, GO solution (30mL, 0.5mg/mL) was mixed carefully with concentrated HNO3 (8ml) and H2SO4 (2ml). Then the mixture was heated and refluxed under microwave irradiation for 2h in a microwave oven operating at a power of 240W. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was kept under mild ultrasonication for a few minutes, and the pH was tuned to 8 with NaOH in an ice-bath. The suspension was filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane to remove the large tracts of GO and a deep yellow solution was separated. At last, the mixture solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 8000–10,000Da) and dried for further use. FT-IR was used to confirm the existence of carboxyl groups.

Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles were prepared according to the reference (Gálvez et al., 2005) with proper modifications. Briefly, to a pH 8 buffered apoferritin solution (5.5mg, 0.16μmol in 0.1M tris/0.15M NaCl) aliquots of CuSO4·5H2O (1mmol, 100μL) were added over a 2h 30min period with 30min intervals between additions. The pH was continuously adjusted to 8 using 0.01M NaOH. The solution was left standing overnight. The obtained Cu2+-apoferritin solution was dialyzed against double distilled deionized water. Then, NaBH4 (50μmol in H2O) was added into the solution of Cu2+-apoferritin (2ml, 4×10−5 M) to obtain the Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles. To confirm the formation of Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles, TEM was used to study the sizes and distributions of nanoparticles.

As shown in Scheme 1(A), at first, Fe3O4 nanospheres were synthesized according to the method in the literature (Deng et al., 2005), which is listed as follows. FeCl3·6H2O (1.35g, 5mmol) was dissolved in ethylene glycol (40mL) to form a clear solution, followed by the addition of NaAc (3.6g) and polyethylene glycol (1.0g). The mixture was stirred vigorously for 30min and then sealed in a teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50mL capacity). The autoclave was heated to and maintained at 200°C for 10h, and allowed to cool to room temperature. The black products were washed several times with ethanol and dried at 60°C for 6h.

Subsequently, amino-function of Fe3O4 nanospheres was performed. Fe3O4 nanospheres (25mg) were re-dispersed in 100mL ethanol by sonication. Then, 70μL 3-(aminopropyl) triethoxysilane was added with mechanical stirring, and the solution was stirred at room temperature overnight. A black precipitate was formed and the precipitate was separated by decantation with the aid of a magnet. The precipitate was washed several times with ethanol and dried in air at room temperature. FT-IR was used to confirm the connection of amino-group onto Fe3O4 nanospheres.

Next, GQDs (2mg/mL) were immersed into EDC (400mM) and NHS (100mM) solutions which were dissolved in water immediately before use to activate the carboxyl groups of GQDs for 4h. Then the amino-functional Fe3O4 nanospheres (10mg) were immersed into the activated GQDs solution and stirred for 6h. The Fe3O4@GQDs hybrid was separated by magnet, washed and re-suspended in 4mL PBS. Then a certain amount of Ab2 and Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles solution was added into the obtained Fe3O4@GQDs hybrid suspension for the attachment. The mixtures were centrifuged at 9000rpm for 15min at 4°C after incubated under gentle stirring for 24h at 4°C. Later, 0.5mL of BSA (2mg/mL) was added into the residual complexes under gentle stirring for about 6h at 4°C to block the unspecified sites of Fe3O4@GQDs/Ab2–Cu-apoferritin bioconjugates and prevent non-specific adsorption between ALVs-J and the bioconjugates. At last, the obtained Fe3O4@GQDs/Ab2–Cu-apoferritin/BSA bioconjugates were collected by centrifugation and redispersed in 1mL PBS, and then stored at 4°C when not use.

A schematic graph of the fabrication process is shown in Scheme 1(B). A bare GCE was polished to a mirror-like surface with 0.3 and 0.05mm alumina slurry on micro-cloth pads and then thoroughly cleaned before use.

Subsequently, 6μL homogeneous suspension of GQDs (2mg/mL) was added onto the bare GCE surface. After the GQDs coated electrode was dried, it was immersed into EDC (400mM) and NHS (100mM) solutions to activate the carboxyl groups of GQDs for 4h. Then after washing with PBS, it was incubated in Ab1 solution at 4°C for 6h. To block the non-specific binding sites, 6μL of 1.0wt% BSA was placed onto the electrode for 1h at 37°C. Then, various concentrations of ALVs-J were incubated with the immobilized Ab1 for 1h at 37°C. Thereafter, the prepared Fe3O4@GQDs/Ab2–Cu-apoferritin/BSA buffer solution was dropped onto the electrode surface and incubated for 1h. After each step, the modified electrode was thoroughly cleaned with PBS. Electrochemical behaviors of the step by step surface modification of the immunosensor were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV). The finished immunosensor was stored at 4°C when not in use.
Before electrochemical detection, the fabricated electrode was immersed into 5mL HCl solution (pH=2) for 20min to release Cu from the cavity of apoferritin.

In order to get well-defined, sharp and highly reproducible stripping peaks for low concentrations of Cu over a low background current a bismuth-coated carbon electrode was applied. Briefly, a polished bare GCE was immersed into a 15mL electrochemical cell, containing 0.1M acetate buffer (pH 4.5) and 400μg/L bismuth. While the solution was stirred, the electrodeposition of bismuth was performed. And the deposition potential was -1.2 V (Wang et al., 2000).

The dissolved Cu nanoparticles were measured with differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) using the bismuth-film electrode. The DPV measurements were performed by a 200s accumulation at −1.2V and subsequent stripping from −0.4V to +0.2V with a step potential of 4mV and amplitude of 50mV; pulse width, pulse period and quiet time were 0.05s, 0.2s and 2s, respectively.

The principle of the immunosensor is illustrated in Scheme 1(B). Quantification of ALVs-J was based on the concentrations of ALVs-J that were proportional to the oxidation peak currents of Cu. It was realized as follows: (i) when ALVs-J were captured onto the Ab1-immobilized surface, the Fe3O4@GQDs/Ab2–Cu-apoferritin/BSA bioconjugates could be immune with ALVs-J specifically, so Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles were introduced on the immunosensor. (ii) When the fabricated electrode was immersed into HCl solution (pH=2), the protein cage of apoferritin could be disassociated into numerous subunits and Cu was released. (iii) The released Cu was oxidized to Cu2+, and at the same time, the oxidation peak currents responses were recorded by DPV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Fe3O4@GQDs hybrid and Cu-apoferritin
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(A) shows a typical TEM image of the synthesized GQDs, the average size of GQDs was 7nm and their size distribution was relatively uniform. FT-IR spectroscopy (shown in Fig. 1(E)) was used to further characterize the functional groups of GQDs. The peaks at 1715 and 1417cm−1 were, respectively attributed to the stretching vibration of C=O and bending vibration of OH, which indicated the existence of carboxyl groups.

Fig. 1(B) displays an overview TEM image of the synthesized Fe3O4 nanospheres. It can be observed that the mean size of the products was about 210nm and they had relatively uniform spherical shape with serration around the spheres. The serrated structure would make Fe3O4@GQDs hybrid combination easier. After the performance of amino-function, FT-IR spectroscopy was used to characterize the introduced amino-group (shown in Fig. 1(F)). The peaks at 1648 and 1593cm−1 were caused by the bending vibration of NH, which indicated the unity of amino-group. As shown in Fig. 1(C), the average particle size of Fe3O4@GQDs was much larger than that of Fe3O4, with a particle size of about 240nm. Meanwhile, we could also observe many small-sized nanoparticles coated on the surface of Fe3O4. The results revealed the formation of Fe3O4@GQDs.

Fig. 1(D) presents the TEM image of Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles. It can be observed that the individual particles are identifiable and the size is 12nm in average; the dense and brunet Cu cores were surrounded by an amorphous protein shell. Then, the concentrations of apoferritin and Cu were determined by the Bradford method and ICP, respectively. The loading amount of Cu was estimated to be 1200 per apoferritin.

Electrochemical behaviors of the step by step surface modification of the GCE in 5mM Fe
(
CN
)
6
3 − / 4 −
(1:1) solution containing 0.1M KCl in potential range of −0.2 to 0.7V with the scan rate of 100mVs−1 are shown in Fig. 2. The bare GCE (curve a) had a pair of well-defined voltammetric peaks. After coated with GQDs (curve b), the peak current decreased. Such phenomenon might be attributed to the abundant oxygen-containing groups on GQDs that hindered the electron transfer of Fe
(
CN
)
6
3 − / 4 −
onto the electrode surface. After attaching Ab1 (curve c), we could see that the current decreased. This is owing to the following two reasons: (i) after activated by EDC/NHS, GQDs could bond many Ab1 via carboxyl groups and (ii) Ab1 hindered the electron transfer. Subsequently, the immunosensor was blocked with BSA and then incubated with ALVs-J, and the redox peaks decreased further (curves d and e). The reasons may be ascribed to the introduction of hydrophobic proteins, which inhibited the electron transfer.

On the other hand, the inset shows comparison of cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE and GQDs modified GCE in 5mM hexaammineruthenium(III) chloride solution in the potential range of −0.35 to 0.2V with the scan rate of 100mVs−1. It can be seen that a couple of typical redox peaks with the peak potential separation (ΔE p) of about 135mV appeared at curve a. In contrast, an increment of the redox peak current and a decrease in the ΔE p (126mV) were observed in curve b. The comparison indicates good conductivity and electrocatalytic effect of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of different signal amplification strategies
ce:section.ce:para: Signal amplification strategies are extremely crucial for immunosensors. In order to evaluate the effect of GQDs and apoferritin for the signal amplification, two other Ab2 (Fe3O4/Ab2–Cu-apoferritin/BSA and Fe3O4@GQDs/Ab2–Cu/BSA) were prepared and the results are shown in Fig. 3. 104.15 TCID50/mL (TCID50: 50% tissue culture infective dose) ALVs-J was used as an example for the evaluation of electrochemical behavior. As indicated in Fig. 3(A), the immunosensor with GQDs (upper curve) could display an increasing current shift (ΔI=19.96μA) than that without GQDs (lower curve). This was mainly attributed to the larger free room GQDs provided for the conjugation of Cu-apoferritin and Ab2. More significantly, in Fig. 3(B), we could find that the electrochemical immunosensor with apoferritin exhibited a higher peak current (ΔI=47.74μA) than that without apoferritin. The reason was attributed to the fact that apoferritin could accommodate a large amount of electroactive probes (Cu). These results confirmed that the use of apoferritin could accommodate more electroactive probes, and GQDs could immobilize more Cu-apoferritin and Ab2. Accordingly, the use of GQDs and apoferritin amplified the signal of the immunosensor greatly.

Under optimal experimental conditions, the prepared immunosensor was examined with different concentrations of ALVs-J. As shown in Fig. 4(A), the currents increased with the concentration of ALVs-J increasing and the peak currents were proportional to the concentration ranging from 102.08 to 104.50 TCID50/mL. The linear regression equation is shown in Fig. 4(B) as I (μA)=37.92logTCID50/mL−75.78 with a correlation coefficient of 0.99955 and a detection limit of 115TCID50/mL (S/N=3). The results demonstrated that the immunosensor had enough sensitivity for monitoring of ALVs-J quantitatively.

To acquire an optimal electrochemical response, accumulation time was an important issue to be considered. We investigated the effect of various accumulation times on the current of the immunosensor using 104.15 TCID50/mL ALVs-J as an example. As shown in Fig. 4(C), the oxidation peak current increased with the increment of accumulation time, finally tending to a steady value after 200s. Therefore, 200s was used in the experiments.

The current responses also depended on the accumulation potential. We investigated the effect of various accumulation potentials on the current of the immunosensor using 104.15 TCID50/mL ALVs-J as an example. As shown in Fig. 4(D), the optimal oxidation peak current was achieved at −1.2V. Thus, −1.2V was chosen for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility and stability of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of the fabricated immunosensor, some possible interferents such as subgroup A of avian leukosis virus (ALVs-A) and avian reticuloendotheliosis virus (AREV) were investigated and compared with ALVs-J. The immunosensor was separately exposed to ALVs-J, ALVs-A and AREV with the concentration of 103.35, 103.65 and 104.15 TCID50/mL, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5(A), the DPV current response of ALVs-J was 13.6 times higher than the response of AREV and 11.8 times than that of ALVs-A (less than 5% in relative error). The results indicated that the selectivity of the immunosensor was acceptable within experimental error.

The reproducibility of the immunosensor was estimated by determining 104.15 TCID50/mL ALVs-J with six immunosensors, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(B). Six immunosensors exhibited similar DPV responses and the RSD was 2.18%. It indicated that the immunosensor showed good reproducibility. The stability of the immunosensor was also examined by periodically examining its current response. After the storage of 30 days, the response currents decreased by about 9.1%. It suggested that the immunosensor possessed acceptable storage stability.

In summary, ultrasensitive electrochemical immunodetection of ALVs-J is achieved by combining GQDs and Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles. The aim of this work was to explore the relevant properties of GQDs and apoferritin. It has been demonstrated that the huge surface area GQDs provided increased the loading of antibodies and Cu-apoferritin nanoparticles, and a large amount of electroactive probes could be encapsulated into the cavity of apoferritin, which increased the electrochemical signal significantly. All the strategies above highly increased the sensitivity of the immunosensor. Moreover, the proposed immunoassay shows excellent analytical performance for the measurement of ALVs-J with low detection limit, high sensitivity, good reproducibility and stability, thus providing a great promise in clinical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21075078 and 21105056) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong province, China (Nos. ZR2010BM005 and ZR2011BQ001).
